article.text: Trump retreat over Hormuz tolls suggests he is struggling to end Iran war Donald Trump's latest Iran War demand lasted all of 24 hours and suggests a president searching for unorthodox ways out of a difficult position. On Monday morning, in a social media post announcing the resumption of an American naval blockade on Iranian shipping, he said that all vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz - including those of US allies - must pay a 20% fee to reimburse the US "for any and all costs necessary to do the job of providing safety and security to this very volatile section of the world". The following day, he abandoned that proposal completely, offering instead that he would strike "trade and investment deals" with America's Gulf allies, implying the US would offer them safe passage through the Strait in return. The abrupt about-face was the latest twist in a conflict that has now lasted more than four months and, despite a month old "memorandum of understanding" that secured a temporary ceasefire and set up a framework for negotiations, shows no sign of ending. Trump may be reluctant to escalate the war given its continued unpopularity, the likelihood of rising energy prices and the risks associated with America forces and allies once again coming under Iranian attack. He might find the prospect of ending the conflict without reaching an agreement he can claim is better than the one Barack Obama's administration negotiated in 2015 also distasteful, however. "I think the most likely ending is a non-ending," Rosemary Kelanic, Director of the Middle East program at Defense Priorities, said. "This has turned into a war of attrition, and wars of attrition tend to go on for a long, long period of time." The US-Iran memorandum of understanding (MOU) - and the hopes that came with it for an end to the war - died at 10:16 ET (16:16 BST) on Tuesday on Truth Social, when Trump announced a resumption of the US blockade of Iranian shipping, amidst a flurry of new US military strikes on targets across Iran. The Iranians countered by stepping up attacks on US allies and commercial shipping in the region, grinding traffic through the Strait of Hormuz to a near standstill once more. After nearly a month of on-again, off-again negotiations between the two nations, punctuated by occasional hostilities that tested the definition of a "ceasefire", Trump and the Americans appear to be facing the same challenges that had been present through much of the Iran War. While militarily, the Americans were achieving their objectives, as measured in Iranian ships, planes and targets destroyed and defence capabilities degraded, politically the conflict was far from resolved. Iran, militarily weakened though it may be, could still deny access to the Strait of Hormuz. And unless the Americans are willing to dramatically escalate their military operations in the region, there was little they could do to stop them. Trump's new twist of a 20% fee - possibly a means of making that military commitment more palatable to the American public - wasn't entirely new. He had suggested such an arrangement on several occasions over the course of the war. But just last month, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio had condemned an Iranian plan to charge "fees" on shipping through Hormuz. "No country is allowed to charge tolls or fees on an international waterway," he said. "That's existing international law. That's the way it is in international waterways all over the world, and that's the way we expect it here." Trump's Hormuz U-turn is just the latest evidence of a president who does not appear to have a clear path forward. The memorandum of understanding, which both Americans and Iranians claimed as a victory for their side, was intentionally vague, leaving much up to later negotiation. The document envisioned some role for Iran in overseeing shipping in Hormuz. It read: "The Islamic Republic of Iran will make arrangements using its best efforts for the safe passage of commercial vessels with no charge." This is a role that Iran has been intent on asserting. The MOU also included billions of dollars in promised "investment" in Iran and an end to international sanctions. Americans may have believed those sweeteners, accompanied by warnings of the consequences of noncompliance, would be enough to dissuade Iran from attempting to use its geographic advantages to more forcefully assert control over Hormuz. That calculation, at least for the moment, appears incorrect. "The MOU is completely dead," said Kelanic. "All of the things that it stipulated have now been undone." Now Trump, and the Iranians, find themselves in a familiar predicament. The latter are once again facing American military attacks across their territory, underlining their inability to defend their territorial sovereignty. With the reimposed blockade, their oil revenue - a lifeline for the Iranian regime - is again cut off. Meanwhile, Trump is again facing a choice between escalation, which comes with domestic economic and political costs, and settling for some kind of resolution that leaves a hostile Iranian regime in power. "We're back to where we were initially, where the question was: who's got more patience?" said Elliot Abrams, senior fellow for Middle Eastern studies at the Council on Foreign Relations. "The Iranians, who will not be able to export oil, or the US and other countries that use Persian gulf oil?" A resumption of full hostilities, or even an escalation in the conflict, would inevitably push oil prices back toward previous highs, endangering that positive trend and again putting Republicans in a tenuous position heading into November's midterm congressional elections. On Monday, after Trump's Truth Social post, the price of a barrel of oil jumped nearly 10% - the biggest one-day increase in six years. The first time around, Trump's blockade helped pressure the Iranians to the negotiating table and set the table for the memorandum of understanding and a framework for a more lasting peace. Now, according to Kelanic, the president's leverage over Iran may be diminished. "He has already tried the things he can easily do, can credibly do," she said. "He can attack military targets, regime targets. He's done that before, and it didn't cause Iran to surrender." The latest target Trump has suggested is Pickaxe Mountain, a heavily fortified nuclear research site south of Tehran. But there is conflicting evidence of the value of the site - or of whether US airstrikes can cause significant damage to the tunnels which are deep beneath granite rock. If Trump's latest moves do ultimately end with yet another ceasefire and face-to-face talks, the underlying, difficult-to-reconcile disagreements - over Hormuz, over the disposition of Iran's nuclear programme, over Iran's influence in the Middle East - remain. "I think there's room for negotiation here over a Strait of Hormuz deal," said Abrams. "But not a return to the MOU." With the war approaching its fifth month, Trump again on Monday noted that other American conflicts - including the Vietnam War - stretched on for years. That particular quagmire, however, hobbled and ultimately ended the presidency of Lyndon Baines Johnson and damaged US global standing for at least a decade. That is a fate Trump is certainly hoping to avoid. His supporters also are weary to repeat the kind of Middle East "forever wars" that Trump condemned in previous presidential campaigns. But with the memorandum of understanding in tatters, the ceasefire ended and the prospect of further conflict looming, the end of the Iran War appears no closer to a resolution than it was in the weeks after it began. Follow the twists and turns of Trump's second term with North America correspondent Anthony Zurcher's weekly US Politics Unspun newsletter. Readers in the UK can sign up here. Those outside the UK can sign up here.

On Monday morning, in a social media post announcing the resumption of an American naval blockade on Iranian shipping, he said that all vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz - including those of US allies - must pay a 20% fee to reimburse the US "for any and all costs necessary to do the job of providing safety and security to this very volatile section of the world".

That's the way it is in international waterways all over the world, and that's the way we expect it here." Trump's Hormuz U-turn is just the latest evidence of a president who does not appear to have a clear path forward.

analysis.title: Trump retreat over Hormuz tolls suggests he is struggling to end Iran war
analysis.summary: 文章分析了特朗普政府在伊朗战争中的政策反复，特别是关于霍尔木兹海峡通行费的提议及其迅速撤回。尽管存在临时停火协议，但美伊双方军事行动持续升级，导致局势陷入僵局。文章指出，特朗普面临国内经济压力、政治选举风险以及军事手段效果有限的多重困境，战争结束的前景依然不明朗。
analysis.main_idea: 特朗普在伊朗战争中的策略摇摆不定，试图通过经济手段（如征收通行费）或军事打击来打破僵局，但均未能取得决定性成果。由于停火协议失效、双方互信破裂以及国内政治经济约束，这场冲突正演变为长期的消耗战，短期内难以找到可持续的解决方案。

vocabulary_targets.meaning_in_context: 指特朗普寻找非传统或非常规的方式来摆脱困境，暗示其策略偏离了常规外交或军事手段。
特朗普声称盟国应支付费用以补偿美国在霍尔木兹海峡提供安全保障的成本。
形容霍尔木兹海峡地区局势不稳定，充满危险和不可预测性。
指特朗普在一天内完全推翻之前的提议，表现出政策立场的突然且彻底的转变。
指美伊之间签署的旨在建立临时停火和谈判框架的谅解备忘录，但随后被证明无效。
指升级战争规模或强度，特朗普因担心后果而对此持谨慎态度。
形容特朗普可能不喜欢在没有达成比2015年协议更优结果的情况下结束冲突。
专家预测战争不会以明确的胜利或和平条约结束，而是陷入无休止的状态。
指通过持续消耗对方资源和耐心来取胜的战争形态，通常持续时间很长。
形容短时间内密集发生的一系列美国军事打击行动。
形容伊朗的反击导致霍尔木兹海峡的交通几乎完全停滞。
指谈判过程被偶尔的敌对行动打断，形容事件发生的节奏。
指伊朗的防御能力被削弱或降低。
指伊朗有能力阻止其他国家通过霍尔木兹海峡。
指特朗普的收费提议可能是为了让美国公众更容易接受这种军事承诺。
美国国务卿强烈反对伊朗对通过海峡的船只收费的计划。
比喻特朗普在霍尔木兹海峡问题上的政策发生了180度的大转弯。
指谅解备忘录的条款不明确，留下了许多待谈判的空间。
指协议中提供的经济利益（如投资和取消制裁），旨在吸引伊朗合作。
美国希望这些利益和警告能阻止伊朗利用地理优势控制海峡。
形容特朗普和伊朗目前所处的尴尬且困难的境地。
指伊朗无法有效保卫其领土主权，面临美国的军事攻击。
比喻石油收入对伊朗政权生存至关重要，如同生命线。
形容共和党在中期选举前因油价上涨而处于脆弱或不稳定的政治地位。
指特朗普对伊朗施加影响或压力的能力，专家认为这种能力已减弱。
指特朗普已经尝试了他能够合理执行且可信的军事手段。
比喻越南战争那样的泥潭，指陷入难以脱身的困境，损害了总统任期。
指越南战争严重阻碍并最终结束了约翰逊的总统任期。
形容谅解备忘录已彻底破裂，如同碎布片，不再有效。
形容进一步的冲突正在逼近，给人带来威胁感。

grammar_targets: Present Perfect Continuous for ongoing actions (e.g., 'has now lasted')
Participle Clauses for concise description (e.g., 'suggesting a president searching...', 'implying the US would offer...')
Passive Voice for formal reporting (e.g., 'was intended', 'are measured')
Modal Verbs for possibility and obligation (e.g., 'must pay', 'might find', 'could still deny')
Contrastive Conjunctions (e.g., 'While militarily... politically...', 'But just last month...')
difficulty_reasons: Contains specialized geopolitical terminology (e.g., 'memorandum of understanding', 'war of attrition', 'sovereignty').
Uses idiomatic expressions and metaphors (e.g., 'abrupt about-face', 'U-turn', 'quagmire', 'sweeteners').
Features complex sentence structures with multiple clauses and embedded phrases.
Requires understanding of nuanced political and military contexts to fully grasp the implications of terms like 'leverage' and 'palatable'.

questions.prompt: What was the specific financial demand Trump initially made regarding shipping in the Strait of Hormuz?

options.text: A 20% fee to reimburse the US for security costs
A 10% tax on all oil exports from Iran
A free passage agreement for US allies only
A donation to the Middle East peace fund

questions.explanation: 文章s2明确指出，特朗普在社交媒体帖子中宣布恢复对伊朗航运的海军封锁，并要求所有通过霍尔木兹海峡的船只支付20%的费用，以补偿美国提供安全保卫的成本。
questions.evidence_quote: he said that all vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz - including those of US allies - must pay a 20% fee to reimburse the US

questions.prompt: Why did the memorandum of understanding (MOU) fail to bring a lasting peace?

options.text: It was intentionally vague and left terms for later negotiation
Iran refused to accept any military inspections
The US withdrew all financial support immediately
It was signed without the approval of Gulf allies

questions.explanation: 文章s20指出，该谅解备忘录是“故意模糊的”（intentionally vague），将许多细节留待后续谈判。s26和s27进一步说明，随着特朗普恢复封锁，MOU中规定的条款已被撤销，导致其彻底失效。
questions.evidence_quote: The memorandum of understanding, which both Americans and Iranians claimed as a victory for their side, was intentionally vague, leaving much up to later negotiation.

questions.prompt: What can be inferred about Trump's leverage over Iran based on the article?

options.text: It has diminished because he has already used his most accessible military options
It has increased due to the successful destruction of Iranian ships
It remains strong because Iran is still dependent on oil exports
It is unaffected by the ongoing military strikes

questions.explanation: 文章s35和s36引用专家Kelanic的观点，指出总统对伊朗的杠杆作用（leverage）可能已经减弱，因为他已经尝试了他能轻易且可信地执行的措施（如攻击军事目标），但这些措施并未导致伊朗投降。
questions.evidence_quote: "He has already tried the things he can easily do, can credibly do," she said.

questions.prompt: In the context of the article, what does the word 'volatile' mean in sentence s2?

options.text: Prone to sudden and unpredictable changes
Rich in natural resources
Politically stable and secure
Geographically isolated

questions.explanation: 在s2中，'volatile'用来形容霍尔木兹海峡地区。结合上下文提到的军事封锁、收费争议以及随后的冲突升级，该地区局势充满危险和不可预测性，因此意为“易变的”或“不稳定的”。
questions.evidence_quote: providing safety and security to this very volatile section of the world

questions.prompt: What is the meaning of 'palatable' in sentence s14?

options.text: Acceptable or pleasing to the public
Expensive and costly
Illegal and controversial
Temporary and short-lived

questions.explanation: s14提到特朗普的收费提议可能是为了让这种军事承诺对“美国公众”更palatable。结合语境，这意味着让公众更容易接受或感到满意，即“可接受的”。
questions.evidence_quote: possibly a means of making that military commitment more palatable to the American public

questions.prompt: Which grammatical structure is used in sentence s11 to contrast military success with political failure?

options.text: A contrastive conjunction ('While... politically...')
A passive voice construction
A conditional clause
A relative clause

questions.explanation: s11使用了“While militarily..., politically...”的结构。'While'在这里作为对比连词，引导让步状语从句，对比了军事上的目标达成与政治上的未解决状态。
questions.evidence_quote: While militarily, the Americans were achieving their objectives, as measured in Iranian ships, planes and targets destroyed and defence capabilities degraded, politically the conflict was far from resolved.

warnings: [warning:option_length_bias] (q6) One option is much longer than another and may reveal the answer
